query: evidence on tumor-associated macrophages as anticancer therapeutic targets

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Alveolar Macrophages -- Clinical Significance -- Role in Cancer Immunotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: In lung cancer, alveolar macrophages can adopt a tumor-promoting phenotype and become "tumor-associated macrophages" (TAMs), which facilitate tumor growth by suppressing antitumor immune responses. Modulating the activity of these macrophages to restore their antitumor function is a promising area in cancer immunotherapy. Several strategies, including immune checkpoint inhibitors and macrophage-targeted therapies, are currently under investigation to harness the power of alveolar macrophages in combating lung cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Krukenberg Tumor -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Huang et al examined the distribution and type of tumor-associated macrophages within primary and metastatic sites in patients with Krukenberg tumor from a gastric primary as an indicator of responsiveness to chemotherapy. Results suggest that Krukenberg tumors are relatively insensitive to chemotherapy and that metastasectomy before chemotherapy improves survival. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Immune-mediated cell destruction is another mechanism by which mAbs have been applied in oncology. This strategy utilized the immune system to target malignant cells and induce destruction through a variety of mechanisms. Examples of such mechanisms include complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC), antibody-dependent cell-mediated phagocytosis (ADCP), and antibody-dependent cellular toxicity (ADCC). These mechanisms are triggered when Fc receptors expressed on endogenous cells (natural killer cells, macrophages, etc.) are activated through binding with Fc receptors on mAbs. Finally, vascular disruption is also a potential target for mAbs in oncological applications. Several mechanisms are possible, including toxin administration to vasculature or stromal cells, stromal cell inhibition, and vascular receptor antagonism. One example of an anti-vascular agent is bevacizumab, a humanized mAb that binds directly to vascular endothelial growth factor-A (VEGF-A) and thereby defers vascular growth in tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Natural Killer Cells -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: While encountering normal cells, inhibitory receptors override activating receptors. However, molecular changes on the cell surface due to viral infection or tumor formation can stimulate the activating receptors. Upon activation, NK cells release granzyme, perforin, effector molecules of the tumor necrosis factor family, and Fas-ligand to induce apoptosis of the target cells. In addition, NK cells synthesize and release other cytokines and chemokines such as Il-10, gamma-interferon, and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor, to recruit other immune effector cells to the target site. NK cells also exhibit antibody-dependent cell cytotoxicity against cells or target antigens which are marked with specific antibodies. Recently, therapeutic applications modulating the inhibition-activation dynamic of NK cells have been developed for multiple conditions including cancer, autoimmune disease, and diabetes. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The main areas described in the literature for the development of bsmAbs include inhibition of multiple cell surface receptors, multiple ligand blockade, receptor cross-linkage, and recruitment of Fc receptor absent T-cells (which would normally not be active with antibody stimulation due to the absence of Fc receptors. The design and implementation of bsmAbs have proven to be challenging despite the conceptually sound nature of the propositions. For example, catumaxomab was one such agent that was initially approved for malignant ascites (2009) but withdrawn from the market in 2017 despite its success due to adverse events. The mechanism of action has been described as trifunctional. There are two antigen-binding regions and an Fc region present on the molecule. The first antigen-binding region targets a trans-membrane glycoprotein named EpCAM, which can be found on malignant cells. The second antigen-binding region binds CD3 antigens on T-cells of the immune system. The Fc region functions to activate and recruit immune cells, including macrophages and natural killer cells, to act against malignant cells. Overall, catumaxomab activates multiple pathways for immune-mediated cell destruction (i.e., ADCC, ADCP, etc.) and cytokine-mediated cytotoxicity. [11] [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen Recognition: The extracellular domain of the CAR is engineered to recognize a specific antigen present on the surface of cancer cells. This antigen is often a tumor-associated antigen (TAA) or a cancer-specific antigen.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Imaging -- Nuclear Medicine -- Emerging Molecular Imaging Targets
retrieved_docs.content: New radiotracers target specific cells and molecules involved in wound pathology. Radiolabeled lymphocytes, macrophages, and fibroblast activation protein inhibitors (FAPI) are under study to detect active fibroblasts and inflammatory responses. Tracers like [68Ga] and [18F]-FAPI show promise in identifying fibroblast activity. Pilot studies exploring bacterial-targeted tracers could soon allow infection detection and bacterial identification, guiding early antibiotic treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Omental Tumors -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: All primary omental tumors are rare, sporadic, and usually malignant; metastatic omental lesions are common. Primary omental tumors are typically derived from adipose, vascular, or lymphatic tissue. Tumor cells and immunologic B, T, and natural killer cells bind to milky spots that may increase tumor growth. [30] [40] [41] [42] [43] [44] Tumor-associated macrophages may also promote cancer progression by secretion of chemokines that promote colonization of tumor cells. Cancer-associated fibroblasts within the omentum may facilitate the spread of cancer through transforming growth factor ß-1, promoting adhesion of cancer cells, and upregulating other growth factors. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Cancer -- Pathophysiology -- Mesothelioma
retrieved_docs.content: High-mobility group box 1 protein, produced by mesothelial cells, contributes to MPM necrosis, inflammation, and carcinogenesis. Tumor necrosis factor-α released by macrophages activates nuclear factor-kappa B signaling, promoting cell survival and proliferation. [28] Asbestos fibers have a large surface area, allowing them to absorb carcinogenic molecules, which further promotes malignancy by increasing exposure to carcinogens within the peritoneal cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Bacillus Calmette–Guérin is a weakened form of Mycobacterium bovis (cowpox). Initially designed as a vaccine for tuberculosis, it is now also used as an immunotherapy for high-grade urothelial cancer. [117] Compared to chemotherapy, BCG has been shown to decrease disease recurrences and reduce progression (up to 37% compared to no BCG therapy) of high-grade urothelial carcinoma. The mechanism of action of BCG against tumor cells is incompletely understood. Originally, it was thought that the immune response to BCG was cross-reacting with bladder tumor cell antigens, but this simplistic mechanism has been largely disproven. Now it appears that BCG has a direct toxic effect on high-grade bladder cancer cells and a local immune-stimulating effect, which allows easier identification of BCG-affected tumor cells by the body's natural anti-tumor cellular defenses (macrophages, natural killer cells, neutrophils, and T lymphocytes.) [201] [202]

retrieved_docs.title: Acid Phosphatase -- Clinical Significance -- Acid Phosphatase as a Therapeutic Target
retrieved_docs.content: Sipuleucel-T is an autologous cellular immunotherapy composed of autologous peripheral blood mononuclear cells, including antigen-presenting cells, activated ex vivo with a recombinant fusion protein of prostatic acid phosphatase linked to granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor. The process involves extracting the patient's autologous dendritic cells by leukapheresis, which are then loaded with Sipuleucel-T ex vivo and re-infused into the patient. The dendritic cells stimulate T cells to target cells expressing prostatic acid phosphatase. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging and Future Immunotherapy Targets
retrieved_docs.content: Several combinatorial strategies have been proposed to have shown promise in developing future therapeutic strategies against tumors that show resistance to currently available strategies. The Ang/Tie 2 pathway is thought to be responsible for basal angiogenesis and vascular stability following vascular endothelial growth factor blockade. [126] Inhibitors such as trebananib, which block this novel pathway, may have a role in combination with VEGF blockade. [127] ALK 1 inhibitors, such as dalantercept, may have a role in interfering with the formation of the vascular bed. [128] Dual mTOR c1/c2 inhibitors may be a potential therapeutic option since the existing therapies only target them on the TOR c1 pathway. [129] Inhibitors targeting hypoxia-inducible factor 2 and aberrant glycolysis, aberrant glutamine, and tryptophan metabolisms, such as CB 839 (glutamine metabolism) indoleamine 2.3 dioxygenase inhibitors, are being developed with an intent to lead to potential additional benefit. [130] [131] It has been suggested that the relationship between the tumor and it’s immune micro-environment needs to be studied further, with the intent to developing beneficial combinatorial therapy options. Other studied approaches include personalized vaccination, targeted radiotherapy to enhance immune responses, cytoreductive surgery following systemic therapy, and immunotherapy or targeted therapy in the neoadjuvant setting. [132] [133] Several molecules that are being investigated as potential targets include chemokine receptors, lymphocyte activation gene – 3, OX -40 (CD 134), B and T lymphocyte attenuator (BTLA), and V-domain containing immunoglobulin containing suppressor of T – lymphocytes activation, soluble. [134]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathology, Inflammation -- Issues of Concern -- Inflammation, Aging, and Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is closely associated with an increased production of ROS and RNOS, which can damage DNA. Chronic inflammation is a process that can increase mutations and cancer risk. Increased cellular or tumor microenvironmental ROS production is associated with diminished control of cell growth. For example, activated macrophages are a major source of ROS, and these inflammatory cells are located in the tumor microenvironment of breast cancer tumors, promoting growth and metastasis. Chronic inflammation is associated with many types of cancer and all stages of cancer. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: T-Cell Activation: Upon binding to the target antigen, the CAR activates the T-cell, leading to its proliferation, cytokine release, and cytotoxic activity against the cancer cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunoglobulin E -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Preclinical research suggests that IgE may have therapeutic potential for treating solid tumors. IgE can augment Fc-mediated effector functions by engaging receptors on immune cells like monocytes, neutrophils, macrophages, eosinophils, basophils, and mast cells. IgE can also engage antigen-presenting cells to enhance antigen uptake and presentation. Antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC) and degranulation result in the release of toxic and pro-inflammatory mediators; these mediators, along with antibody-dependent cell-mediated phagocytosis (ADCP), enhance anti-tumor activity. IgE can also engage antigen-presenting cells to improve antigen uptake and presentation. IgE impacts cancer cells, like anti-cancer IgG antibodies, decreasing cancer cell growth signaling. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophage facilitation of metastasis: Tumor-associated macrophages (TAM), especially those in the stroma, facilitate tumor development, progression, and the eventual seeding of metastasis. Researchers do not consider them neoplastic. However, many human metastatic tumors also contain neoplastic cells with macrophage properties. It is not possible to differentiate neoplastic macrophages from non-neoplastic ones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: agents that damage DNA and inhibit tumor cell replication; doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation are anthracycline antibiotics that intercalate DNA and generate free radicals; streptozocin is an alkylating antimetabolite that interferes with carbohydrate metabolism in malignant cells; vinblastine sulfate and vinorelbine tartrate are vinca‑alkaloid microtubule inhibitors that block mitosis; pembrolizumab is an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor that enhances T‑cell–mediated antitumor immunity; and sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor that augments immune responses. All of these agents may be employed in the multimodal treatment of Hodgkin disease, reflecting a therapeutic strategy that combines cytotoxic chemotherapy, targeted immunotherapy, and immune modulation to improve disease control and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Conventional alkylating and antimetabolite chemotherapies—chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, mechlorethamine, procarbazine (including its hydrochloride form), dakarbazin and lomustine—exert cytotoxic effects on rapidly dividing Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Platinum‑based therapy with cisplatin and anthracycline antibiotics doxorubicin hydrochloride (both standard and liposomal formulations) add DNA‑damaging mechanisms. Additional cytotoxic agents used in Hodgkin protocols include bleomycin sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate and streptozocin, each targeting distinct aspects of tumor proliferation and survival. Targeted and immunomodulatory drugs expand therapeutic options: brentuximab vedotin delivers an anti‑CD30 antibody‑drug conjugate directly to Hodgkin lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: and chlormethin hydrochlorid further contribute to the chemotherapeutic arsenal. Targeted and immunomodulatory therapies are highlighted by brentuximab vedotin (a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate), pembrolizumab (a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor) and sargramostim (a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor), each directing immune mechanisms against Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a multimodal treatment paradigm that integrates alkylators, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, antimitotics, antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies and immune‑stimulating agents—used alone or in combination—to manage Hodgkin disease, underscoring the importance of diverse chemotherapy regimens and emerging immunotherapies in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
retrieved_docs.content: Vinblastine, vinorelbine, chlorambucil, dacarbazine, procarbazine, chlormethine hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride, ifosfamide, streptozocin, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, and vinorelbine tartrate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease. The majority of these compounds are conventional cytotoxic chemotherapeutics—vinblastine, vinorelbine, vinorelbine tartrate, dacarbazine, procarbazine, chlormethine hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride, ifosfamide, chlorambucil, and streptozocin—reflecting the central role of chemotherapy in managing this lymphoid malignancy. In addition, pembrolizumab, a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune‑checkpoint inhibitor, and sargramostim, a granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, represent immunomodulatory approaches that may be employed, particularly in relapsed or refractory Hodgkin disease. Together, these diverse therapeutic options illustrate the multimodal strategy—combining traditional chemotherapy with targeted immunotherapy—used to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes in Hodgkin disease.

retrieved_docs.content: anhydrous, ifosfamide, lomustin, mechlorethamine, procarbazine hydrochloride, chlormethin hydrochloride and streptozocin are primarily alkylating or antineoplastic agents; doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation are anthracycline chemotherapeutics; vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate and vinorelbine tartrate are vinca‑alkaloid antimitotics; brentuximab vedotin is an antibody‑drug conjugate targeting CD30‑positive malignancies; pembrolizumab is a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor used in immunotherapy; sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor that promotes white‑blood‑cell recovery; dakarbazin, chlormethin hydrochloride and procarbazine hydrochloride have more specialized oncologic uses. Collectively, these pharmacologic substances are employed in multiple chemotherapy regimens and targeted‑therapy protocols for the treatment of Hodgkin lymphoma, providing disease control, remission induction and survival benefit through mechanisms

Immunostimulants are further classified as tiloron agents, indicating that tiloron represents a broader therapeutic class encompassing immunostimulatory compounds. This integrated hierarchy of hormones, extracellular‑matrix components, enzymatic families, oncogenic biomarkers, adjuvants, and qualifier‑based descriptors provides a comprehensive framework for hormonal regulation, matrix biology, immunomodulation, biomarker evaluation, and precise therapeutic categorization in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, ifosfamid, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbin tartarát are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease. The list includes classic cytotoxic chemotherapy drugs such as vincristine, bleomycin, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin (both conventional and liposomal formulations), ifosfamide, vinblastine, and vinorelbine, as well as the alkylating agent procarbazine (hydrochlorid prokarbazinu). In addition, newer therapeutic options are represented by pembrolizumab, an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor targeting PD‑1, and sargramostim, a granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor that can modulate immune responses. Together, these diverse pharmacologic classes provide multiple mechanisms of action for managing Hodgkin disease, ranging from direct tumor cell cytotoxicity to immune‑based strategies, underscoring the importance of a multimodal approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: providing a targeted therapy for Hodgkin lymphoma and systemic anaplastic large‑cell lymphoma. Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy agent that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, while its liposomal formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome) offers altered pharmacokinetics and reduced cardiotoxicity for various solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immunity in a wide range of cancers, including melanoma, non‑small cell lung cancer, and urothelial carcinoma. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) used to accelerate recovery of white blood cells after chemotherapy or bone‑marrow transplantation. Vinblastine sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát are vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, with vinblastine commonly used for Hodgkin lymphoma and testicular cancer, and vinorelbine

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host immunity. Collectively, these agents—ranging from traditional chemotherapeutics and central‑nervous‑system depressants to modern immunotherapies—illustrate the comprehensive pharmacologic approach to Hodgkin disease, emphasizing individualized regimen selection to optimize patient outcomes and underscoring the importance of systematic drug classification for clinical research and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is a broad category that includes a diverse array of agents such as ethinylestradiol, barbiturates, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, lomustine, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, and vinblastine sulfate. These compounds represent multiple therapeutic classes: ethinylestradiol is a synthetic estrogen used in hormonal therapy; barbiturates are central‑nervous‑system depressants; bleomycin sulfate, ifosfamide, vinblastine sulfate, and lomustine are cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents; brentuximab vedotin and pembrolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target specific cancer antigens and immune checkpoints; sargramostim is a recombinant cytokine that stimulates granulocyte‑macrophage colony formation; and chlormethin hydrochloride and procarbazine hydrochloride are alkylating agents used in oncology. All of these substances are recorded as values for a specific data‑catalog property associated with the Pharmacologic Substance class, highlighting their shared classification while underscoring their distinct mechanisms of action and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is a broad class that includes diverse therapeutic agents such as the synthetic estrogen Ethinylestradiol, the anticoagulant Semuloparin, the investigational cell‑adhesion molecule inhibitor Uproleselan, and the barbiturate subclass (Barbituráty). Cytotoxic and anticancer agents represented in this class include the alkylating agents Chlorambucil, Cyclophosphamide anhydrous, Ifosfamid, Mechlorethamine (Mechlorethamin), Procarbazine (Prokarbazin), Chlormethin hydrochloride, Cisplatin (Cisplatina), Dakarbazin, Lomustine (Lomustin) and Bleomycin sulfate; anthracycline antibiotics Doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation; the platinum‑based drug Cisplatin; the antimetabolite Streptozocin; and the vinca‑alkaloids Vincristine sulfate, Vinblastine sulfate and Vinorelbine tartrate. Immunomodulatory and targeted agents include the recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor Sargramostim, the PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor Pembrolizumab, and the antibody‑drug conjugate Brentuximab vedotin. Collectively, these substances illustrate the extensive range of pharmacologic substances—from hormones and anticoagulants to barbiturates, chemotherapeutics, immunotherapies and investigational agents—and they constitute the diverse pharmacologic strategies employed in the management of Hodgkin lymphoma, where alkylating agents, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, vinca‑alkaloids, bleomycin, dakarbazin, lomustine, brentuximab vedotin, pembrolizumab, sargramostim and streptozocin are all reported as potential therapeutic options.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1485
retrieved_docs.content: Cisplatin, mechlorethamine, procarbazine, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, doxorubicin hydrochloride, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, sargramostim, streptozocin, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease. These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including traditional alkylating chemotherapies (mechlorethamine, procarbazine, cisplatin), antitumor antibiotics (doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation), antitumor antibiotics with a different mechanism (bleomycin sulfate), a nucleoside analog (streptozocin), a vinca‑alkaloid derivative (vinorelbine tartrate), a recombinant cytokine that stimulates granulocyte‑macrophage colony formation (sargramostim), and a targeted antibody‑drug conjugate directed against CD30 (brentuximab vedotin). Their inclusion as possible treatments reflects the multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, where combinations of cytotoxic chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunomodulation are employed to achieve optimal disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic Substance is a broad therapeutic class that encompasses a wide variety of chemical agents and biologics, including the synthetic estrogen ethinylestradiol, the barbiturate subclass barbituráty, and ethanol, which also appears as an excipient in coal‑tar 50 MG/ML medicated shampoo. Within this class, numerous antineoplastic and cytotoxic agents are represented, such as bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlorambucil, chlormethin hydrochlorid, cisplatin (cisplatina), cyclophosphamide anhydrous, dakarbazin, doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, procarbazine (hydrochlorid prokarbazinu), ifosfamid, lomustine (lomustin), mechlorethamine, streptozocin, as well as the vinca‑alkaloids vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate and vinorelbine tartrate. Immunomodulatory and biologic therapies in this group include the checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab and the recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor sargramostim. Collectively, these agents constitute the diverse pharmacologic strategies—alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamid, procarbazine), platinum‑based therapy (cisplatin), nitrogen‑mustard derivatives (dakarbazin, lomustine), antimetabolites (mechlorethamine), vinca‑alkaloids (vinblastine sulfate, vincristine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), anthracyclines (doxorubicin hydrochloride and liposomal form), bleomycin, monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), antihistamine‑related agent (chlormethin hydrochlorid), immune checkpoint inhibition (pembrolizumab), and cytokine therapy (sargramostim). These pharmacologic substances are employed, often in combination, for the treatment of Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين), illustrating the integration of traditional chemotherapy with targeted and immunotherapeutic approaches in modern lymphoma management.
